nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, lakes, and floodplains with dense aquatic vegetation
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical freshwater; benthopelagic; prefers areas with floating vegetation

diet.notes: Microphagous filter-feeder by nature; tends to graze on algae, biofilm, and soft plant matter. Has been documented accepting quality flake foods, small pellets, Spirulina-based foods, and live or frozen items such as daphnia, bloodworms, brine shrimp, and mosquito larvae. Vegetable matter (blanched lettuce, zucchini, spinach) is generally well received.

imageCandidates.notes: Green Yoshi; 4223x2699. Sharp side profile of the pink leucistic trade form; whole fish in frame with the diagnostic thick fleshy protrusible lips clearly shown against a dark background.
5snake5; 4272x2412. Sharp shot of the wild green/olive form, whole fish and fleshy lips visible; chosen to show the natural colour form alongside the pink morph.
Aditi Jivani; 6000x4000. Two pink leucistic individuals together in a planted aquarium, illustrating how the species is kept in pairs; slight fin motion but both fish crisp and well framed.

summary: The Kissing Gourami (Helostoma temminkii) is a large Southeast Asian labyrinth fish known for its lip-to-lip displays, which are territorial rather than affectionate. Adults can reach 30 cm and need a spacious aquarium. The pink form is a selectively bred leucistic variant.
careNotes: The "kissing" lip-to-lip contact this species is named for has been documented as a territorial or dominance display — not affectionate behavior. The pink form widely sold in the trade is a selectively-bred leucistic morph; the wild green/olive form is rarely encountered commercially. Short-bodied ("balloon") variants tend to have significantly shorter lifespans and are generally not recommended. This species is atypical among gouramis in being an egg-scatterer rather than a bubble-nester. It grows large (up to 30 cm) and tends to become territorial as it matures, so a tank of at least 200 L with a 150 cm footprint is generally advised. Groups should ideally be introduced simultaneously as juveniles; pairs can be aggressive toward each other with limited space. Possesses a labyrinth organ and needs access to the water surface to breathe atmospheric air; the air gap should be kept warm to avoid respiratory stress. A secure lid is advised.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported as rarely achieved by aquarists. Spawning occurs near the surface under floating vegetation; the male wraps around the female and thousands of buoyant eggs are scattered — no parental care follows. Eggs have been reported to hatch within 24–36 hours. Fry require infusoria or very fine foods initially. Sexual maturity is typically reached at around 120–150 mm standard length.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, range, water parameters, tank dimensions, behavioral notes, and breeding.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family, order, native range, temperature (22–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), adult size (30 cm). Confirmed minimum tank length of 150 cm. Minor disagreement on max temp (28°C vs SeriouslyFish 30°C) — used 28°C as the average of all three sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (up to 12 in / ~30 cm), temperature (72–82°F / 22–28°C), pH (6–8), hardness (5–20 dGH), minimum tank (50 gal / ~189 L for a pair), semi-aggressive temperament, lifespan (5–7 years, some to 25 years), omnivore diet, and egg-scattering breeding behavior. Min tank biased toward 200 L over the stated 189 L per playbook caution rule given large adult size and territorial behavior.

fish.breedingNotes: The Kissing Gourami is an egg-scatterer that spawns near the surface beneath floating vegetation, scattering thousands of buoyant eggs with no parental care following. Successful breeding in captivity has been reported only rarely. Eggs have been documented hatching within 24–36 hours.
